title: New England lobster roll
subtitle: Cold lobster, lemon mayonnaise, buttered split-top bun.
excerpt: Picked lobster meat folded with mayonnaise, lemon, and chive, piled into a buttered split-top bun. Maine-style: cold filling, hot bun. The clam-shack lunch of the Atlantic seaboard in twenty minutes once the lobster is cooked.
sourceNotes: The split-top hot-dog bun became the lobster-roll vehicle in Maine in the 1920s, documented in the local clam-shack tradition. The mayonnaise binding follows the cold-poultry-salad logic of early-twentieth-century American cookery (Fannie Farmer).

content.text: Two whole live lobsters of around 700 g each give you 400 g of picked meat: claws, knuckles, and tails. The meat goes cold straight away into iced water to stop the cooking, then gets dressed with mayonnaise, the juice of half a lemon, and a small handful of chives. The bun is a split-top hot-dog roll: butter the flat sides and toast them on a griddle until deep gold. Cold filling, hot bun, eaten the moment it is assembled.

The Maine version is cold and mayonnaise-bound. The Connecticut version is warm and butter-dressed, an equally fierce regional pride; the technique below leads with Maine but switches with one line of method changes.
What you need

items.name: Lobster
items.prepNote: live, ~700 g each, or 400 g picked cooked lobster meat

items.name: Fine sea salt
items.prepNote: for the boiling water, 30 g per litre, sea-water salinity

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: split-top hot-dog buns, or brioche rolls split flat-sided

content.text: A tall stockpot of 8 litres or more for the lobsters. A heavy frying pan or griddle for the buns. Kitchen scissors for cracking the shells, faster than a nutcracker and easier to control.
Method
Cook the lobsters

Bring 4 litres of water to a fierce boil in the stockpot. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Lower the lobsters head-first into the pot, cover, and bring back to the boil. Time from when the boil returns: 8 minutes for a 700 g lobster, 10 minutes for a kilo. Lift out with tongs and plunge into a sink of cold water with plenty of ice. Cool fully, 10 minutes.
Pick the meat

Twist the claws off at the body. Crack each claw with the back of a heavy knife or the scissors. Pull the knuckles apart and prise the meat out with a skewer. Twist the tail off; cut down each side with scissors and lift the shell off; pull the meat out in one piece and check for the dark intestinal thread, removing if present. Chop the tail meat into 1 cm pieces; leave the claw meat whole.
Dress the lobster

In a medium bowl, whisk {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise with the juice of {{lemon}} of lemon. Add the picked lobster, {{chives}} of chopped chives, the finely chopped celery if using, {{sea-salt-flakes}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Fold gently to coat. Cover and refrigerate for 30 minutes; this lets the dressing settle.
Toast the buns

Heat a frying pan over medium heat. Butter the flat outer sides of each split-top bun generously with {{salted-butter}} of softened butter. Lay them in the pan and toast for 2 minutes a side until deep gold and crisp.
Assemble
Open each bun, lay a leaf of baby gem in the cradle if using, then pile in a quarter of the lobster mixture. Eat at once.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Drain the picked meat on kitchen paper for 5 minutes before dressing. The cold-water bath also stops the meat continuing to cook.
items.cause: Not chilled and dried after picking.

items.fix: Always use both claw and tail meat. Season with salt flakes at dressing time and re-taste; the cold-water plunge dulls the seasoning.
items.cause: Under-seasoned, or all white-meat tail used.

items.fix: Use a split-top hot-dog bun where the cut goes through the top, not the side. The two flat outer surfaces are what toast properly.
Time from when the water returns to a boil. 8 minutes for 700 g, no longer. Pull the lobsters out the moment the timer goes, not when you remember.

content.text: Variations

The Connecticut version skips the mayonnaise entirely. Pile the lobster meat into a warmed pan with 80 g of melted butter, toss to coat, and stuff into the toasted bun straight from the heat. A crab roll uses 300 g of picked white crab meat dressed exactly as the lobster. A prawn version with 400 g of cooked peeled prawns works as the weekday substitute when fresh lobster is dear.
Where this dish lives

Lobster rolls are the lunch of every roadside clam shack along the coast from Cape Cod up through Maine and into the Canadian Maritimes. The Maine and Connecticut camps argue this annually and neither side ever changes its mind. Eat with a paper plate, a bottle of cold beer, a view of the water, and a pile of paper napkins.
